Raw code at below. ----------------- /** * Common properties for vector stores. * * @author Josh Long * @author Soby Chacko */ public class CommonVectorStoreProperties { /** * Vector stores do not initialize schema by default on application startup. The * applications explicitly need to opt-in for initializing the schema on startup. The * recommended way to initialize the schema on startup is to set the initialize-schema * property on the vector store. See {@link #setInitializeSchema(boolean)}. */ private boolean initializeSchema = false; public boolean isInitializeSchema() { return this.initializeSchema; } public void setInitializeSchema(boolean initializeSchema) { this.initializeSchema = initializeSchema; } } ----------------- Documentation contents at below. ----------------- Deleting by ID list is generally faster when you know exactly which documents to remove. Filter-based deletion may require scanning the index to find matching documents; however, this is vector store implementation-specific. Large deletion operations should be batched to avoid overwhelming the system. Consider using filter expressions when deleting based on document properties rather than collecting IDs first. To connect to MongoDB Atlas and use the MongoDBAtlasVectorStore, you need to provide access details for your instance. A simple configuration can be provided via Spring Boot’s application.yml: spring: data: mongodb: uri: <mongodb atlas connection string> database: <database name> ai: vectorstore: mongodb: initialize-schema: true collection-name: custom_vector_store index-name: custom_vector_index path-name: custom_embedding metadata-fields-to-filter: author,year Properties starting with spring.ai.vectorstore.mongodb.* are used to configure the MongoDBAtlasVectorStore: To connect to OpenSearch and use the OpenSearchVectorStore, you need to provide access details for your instance. A simple configuration can be provided via Spring Boot’s application.yml: spring: ai: vectorstore: opensearch: uris: <opensearch instance URIs> username: <opensearch username> password: <opensearch password> index-name: spring-ai-document-index initialize-schema: true similarity-function: cosinesimil read-timeout: <time to wait for response> connect-timeout: <time to wait until connection established> path-prefix: <custom path prefix> ssl-bundle: <name of SSL bundle> aws: # Only for Amazon OpenSearch Service host: <aws opensearch host> service-name: <aws service name> access-key: <aws access key> secret-key: <aws secret key> region: <aws region> Properties starting with spring.ai.vectorstore.opensearch.* are used to configure the OpenSearchVectorStore:

You can control whether the AWS-specific OpenSearch auto-configuration is enabled using the spring.ai.vectorstore.opensearch.aws.enabled property.

  • If this property is set to false, the non-AWS OpenSearch configuration is activated, even if AWS SDK classes are present on the classpath. This allows you to use self-managed or third-party OpenSearch clusters in environments where AWS SDKs are present for other services.

  • If AWS SDK classes are not present, the non-AWS configuration is always used.

  • If AWS SDK classes are present and the property is not set or set to true, the AWS-specific configuration is used by default.

This fallback logic ensures that users have explicit control over the type of OpenSearch integration, preventing accidental activation of AWS-specific logic when not desired.

The path-prefix property allows you to specify a custom path prefix when OpenSearch is running behind a reverse proxy that uses a non-root path. For example, if your OpenSearch instance is accessible at https://example.com/opensearch/ instead of https://example.com/, you would set path-prefix: /opensearch.

The following similarity functions are available: cosinesimil - Default, suitable for most use cases. Measures cosine similarity between vectors. l1 - Manhattan distance between vectors. l2 - Euclidean distance between vectors. linf - Chebyshev distance between vectors. Create a new Spring Boot project and add the spring-ai-starter-model-openai to your pom (or gradle) dependencies. Add a application.properties file, under the src/main/resources directory, to enable and configure the OpenAi chat model: spring.ai.openai.api-key=${NVIDIA_API_KEY} spring.ai.openai.base-url=https://integrate.api.nvidia.com spring.ai.openai.chat.options.model=meta/llama-3.1-70b-instruct # The NVIDIA LLM API doesn't support embeddings, so we need to disable it. spring.ai.openai.embedding.enabled=false # The NVIDIA LLM API requires this parameter to be set explicitly or server internal error will be thrown. spring.ai.openai.chat.options.max-tokens=2048 replace the api-key with your NVIDIA credentials. NVIDIA LLM API requires the max-token parameter to be explicitly set or server error will be thrown. Here is an example of a simple @Controller class that uses the chat model for text generations. @RestController public class ChatController { private final OpenAiChatModel chatModel; @Autowired public ChatController(OpenAiChatModel chatModel) { this.chatModel = chatModel; } @GetMapping("/ai/generate") public Map generate(@RequestParam(value = "message", defaultValue = "Tell me a joke") String message) { return Map.of("generation", this.chatModel.call(message)); } @GetMapping("/ai/generateStream") public Flux<ChatResponse> generateStream(@RequestParam(value = "message", defaultValue = "Tell me a joke") String message) { Prompt prompt = new Prompt(new UserMessage(message)); return this.chatModel.stream(prompt); } } You can use the following properties in your Spring Boot configuration to customize the Milvus vector store. The OpenAiSdkEmbeddingOptions.java provides the OpenAI configurations, such as the model to use, dimensions, and user identifier. The default options can be configured using the spring.ai.openai-sdk.embedding.options properties as well. At start-time use the OpenAiSdkEmbeddingModel constructor to set the default options used for all embedding requests. At run-time you can override the default options, using a OpenAiSdkEmbeddingOptions instance as part of your EmbeddingRequest. For example to override the default model name for a specific request: EmbeddingResponse embeddingResponse = embeddingModel.call( new EmbeddingRequest(List.of("Hello World", "World is big and salvation is near"), OpenAiSdkEmbeddingOptions.builder() .model("text-embedding-3-large") .dimensions(1024) .build())); In addition to the model specific OpenAiSdkEmbeddingOptions you can use a portable EmbeddingOptions instance, created with the builder. The OpenAiChatOptions.java class provides model configurations such as the model to use, the temperature, the frequency penalty, etc. On start-up, the default options can be configured with the OpenAiChatModel(api, options) constructor or the spring.ai.openai.chat.options.* properties. description: This class, `VectorStoreRuntimeHintsTests`, is designed to test the registration of runtime hints specifically for vector stores. It verifies that the hints are correctly registered with the `RuntimeHints` object, which is a core component of Spring's AOT support. It ensures the necessary ANTLR grammar file (`Filters.g4`) is included for vector store filtering functionality.
exampleUsage: The primary use case is to ensure that the vector store components are correctly configured for AOT compilation. The tests verify that the `VectorStoreRuntimeHints` class correctly registers the required resources (specifically the ANTLR grammar file) with the `RuntimeHints` object. description: The `FilterExpressionTextParser` class parses a textual filter expression language (similar to SQL WHERE clauses) into a `Filter.Expression` object. It uses an ANTLR4 grammar to define the language and provides caching for performance. It handles various operators like AND, OR, IN, NIN, equals, greater than, less than, and more.
relationship: This class is used by components like `SearchRequest` (via its Builder) to parse filter expressions provided as strings. It interacts with the `Filter` interface and its related types (`Filter.Expression`, `Filter.Operand`, etc.) to represent the parsed filter. It's a core component for enabling metadata filtering in vector search operations.
exampleUsage:
1. **Parsing a filter expression:** `FilterExpressionTextParser parser = new FilterExpressionTextParser(); Filter.Expression expression = parser.parse("country == 'BG' && year >= 2020");`
2. **Using in a SearchRequest:** `SearchRequest request = SearchRequest.builder().query("some query").filterExpression("country == 'US'").build();`
3. **Clearing the cache:** `parser.clearCache();` (useful for testing or when filter expressions are dynamically updated).

description: The `Filter` class provides a portable way to generate metadata filter expressions for vector stores. It allows building complex filters using operations like equality, inequality, greater than, less than, IN, and NOT, combined with AND and OR logic. These expressions can then be converted into vector-store specific native expressions.
relationship: This class is used by components like `VectorStoreDocumentRetriever` and `SearchRequest` to filter documents based on metadata. It interacts with various vector store implementations (MongoDBAtlasVectorStore, OracleVectorStore, Milvus, Weaviate) through the `filterExpression` parameter in `similaritySearch` methods. It also works with `FilterExpressionBuilder` for programmatic filter creation and `FilterExpressionTextParser` for parsing filter expressions from strings.
exampleUsage:
The primary use case is to define filters for retrieving documents from vector stores.  You can create filters programmatically using `FilterExpressionBuilder`:

```java
FilterExpressionBuilder b = new FilterExpressionBuilder();
Expression expression = b.eq("country", "BG").build();
```

Or, you can use a string-based expression:

```java
vectorStore.similaritySearch(
    SearchRequest.builder()
    .query("The World")
    .filterExpression("author in ['john', 'jill'] && article_type == 'blog'").build());
```

Common operators include `EQ`, `NE`, `GT`, `GTE`, `LT`, `LTE`, `IN`, `NIN`, `AND`, and `OR`.

description: This interface defines a visitor for the parse tree generated by the `FiltersParser`, which is used to process filter expressions. description: This class, `FilterExpressionVisitor`, is a visitor pattern implementation that parses filter expressions (defined using ANTLR4) and converts them into a `Filter.Operand` hierarchy. exampleUsage:

This guide helps you migrate from the deprecated FunctionCallback API to the new ToolCallback API in Spring AI. For more information about the new APIs, check out the Tools Calling documentation.

Embeddings are numerical representations of text, images, or videos that capture relationships between inputs.

Embeddings work by converting text, image, and video into arrays of floating point numbers, called vectors. These vectors are designed to capture the meaning of the text, images, and videos. The length of the embedding array is called the vector’s dimensionality.

By calculating the numerical distance between the vector representations of two pieces of text, an application can determine the similarity between the objects used to generate the embedding vectors.

The EmbeddingModel interface is designed for straightforward integration with embedding models in AI and machine learning. Its primary function is to convert text into numerical vectors, commonly referred to as embeddings. These embeddings are crucial for various tasks such as semantic analysis and text classification.

The design of the EmbeddingModel interface centers around two primary goals:

  • Portability: This interface ensures easy adaptability across various embedding models. It allows developers to switch between different embedding techniques or models with minimal code changes. This design aligns with Spring’s philosophy of modularity and interchangeability.

  • Simplicity: EmbeddingModel simplifies the process of converting text to embeddings. By providing straightforward methods like embed(String text) and embed(Document document), it takes the complexity out of dealing with raw text data and embedding algorithms. This design choice makes it easier for developers, especially those new to AI, to utilize embeddings in their applications without delving deep into the underlying mechanics.

To connect to OpenSearch and use the OpenSearchVectorStore, you need to provide access details for your instance. A simple configuration can be provided via Spring Boot’s application.yml: spring: ai: vectorstore: opensearch: uris: <opensearch instance URIs> username: <opensearch username> password: <opensearch password> index-name: spring-ai-document-index initialize-schema: true similarity-function: cosinesimil read-timeout: <time to wait for response> connect-timeout: <time to wait until connection established> path-prefix: <custom path prefix> ssl-bundle: <name of SSL bundle> aws: # Only for Amazon OpenSearch Service host: <aws opensearch host> service-name: <aws service name> access-key: <aws access key> secret-key: <aws secret key> region: <aws region> Properties starting with spring.ai.vectorstore.opensearch.* are used to configure the OpenSearchVectorStore:

You can control whether the AWS-specific OpenSearch auto-configuration is enabled using the spring.ai.vectorstore.opensearch.aws.enabled property.

  • If this property is set to false, the non-AWS OpenSearch configuration is activated, even if AWS SDK classes are present on the classpath. This allows you to use self-managed or third-party OpenSearch clusters in environments where AWS SDKs are present for other services.

  • If AWS SDK classes are not present, the non-AWS configuration is always used.

  • If AWS SDK classes are present and the property is not set or set to true, the AWS-specific configuration is used by default.

This fallback logic ensures that users have explicit control over the type of OpenSearch integration, preventing accidental activation of AWS-specific logic when not desired.

The path-prefix property allows you to specify a custom path prefix when OpenSearch is running behind a reverse proxy that uses a non-root path. For example, if your OpenSearch instance is accessible at https://example.com/opensearch/ instead of https://example.com/, you would set path-prefix: /opensearch.

The following similarity functions are available: cosinesimil - Default, suitable for most use cases. Measures cosine similarity between vectors. l1 - Manhattan distance between vectors. l2 - Euclidean distance between vectors. linf - Chebyshev distance between vectors. A Redis Stack instance EmbeddingModel instance to compute the document embeddings. Several options are available: The vector database’s role is to store and facilitate similarity searches for these embeddings. It does not generate the embeddings itself. For creating vector embeddings, the EmbeddingModel should be utilized. The similaritySearch methods in the interface allow for retrieving documents similar to a given query string. These methods can be fine-tuned by using the following parameters: k: An integer that specifies the maximum number of similar documents to return. This is often referred to as a 'top K' search, or 'K nearest neighbors' (KNN). threshold: A double value ranging from 0 to 1, where values closer to 1 indicate higher similarity. By default, if you set a threshold of 0.75, for instance, only documents with a similarity above this value are returned. Filter.Expression: A class used for passing a fluent DSL (Domain-Specific Language) expression that functions similarly to a 'where' clause in SQL, but it applies exclusively to the metadata key-value pairs of a Document. filterExpression: An external DSL based on ANTLR4 that accepts filter expressions as strings. For example, with metadata keys like country, year, and isActive, you could use an expression such as: country == 'UK' && year >= 2020 && isActive == true. Find more information on the Filter.Expression in the Metadata Filters section.

1. **Adding and Retrieving Documents:** Demonstrates adding a single document or multiple documents and then retrieving them using similarity search.
2. **Saving and Loading:** Shows how to save the vector store to a file and load it back, ensuring data persistence.
3. **Similarity Search with Threshold:** Illustrates using a similarity threshold to filter search results, returning only documents above a certain similarity score.
4. **Handling Edge Cases:** Tests the store's behavior with empty or null document lists, and deletion of non-existent documents.

description: This class is a parser generated by ANTLR4, designed to parse filter expressions for metadata filtering in a vector database. It defines the grammar for a 'where' clause-like syntax to filter documents based on their metadata key-value pairs.
relationship: This class is used in conjunction with the `EmbeddingModel` and `VectorDatabase` components. The `EmbeddingModel` generates embeddings, the `VectorDatabase` stores them, and `FiltersParser` is used to parse filter expressions that refine similarity searches within the `VectorDatabase` based on document metadata.
exampleUsage: The primary use case is parsing filter expressions provided as strings. For example, a filter expression like `country == 'UK' && year >= 2020 && isActive == true` would be parsed by this class to enable filtering of documents based on these criteria. The parsed expression is then used to filter results from the vector database's similarity search.

description: The TranslationQueryTransformer class translates a user query to a target language supported by the embedding model, using a large language model. It returns the original query if it's already in the target language or if the source language is unknown.
relationship: This class is a component used in the pre-retrieval phase of a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline. It implements the QueryTransformer interface and works with ChatClient to perform the translation. It's part of a larger ETL pipeline for processing data and preparing it for AI models.

description: This class, `ConstantArrayContext`, represents the context of a constant array within a grammar defined by `FiltersParser`. It provides access to the tokens that make up the array, including the opening and closing square brackets, commas separating the constants, and the constant values themselves.
relationship: This class is part of a parser generated by ANTLR4, specifically for the `FiltersParser`. It's a rule context, meaning it represents a specific production rule (constant array) in the grammar. It interacts with other contexts like `ConstantContext` (representing individual constants) and is used by listeners and visitors (`FiltersListener`, `FiltersVisitor`) to process the parsed filter expressions.
exampleUsage: This class is primarily used internally by the parser to represent the structure of a constant array during parsing.  It's not directly instantiated or used by application code. Instead, it's used by the parser to provide a structured representation of the input filter expression, which can then be traversed by listeners or visitors to perform actions like validation or code generation. The `constant()` methods are used to access the individual constant values within the array.

description: This class, `InNinTestConverter`, extends `PrintFilterExpressionConverter` and overrides the `doExpression` method to handle `IN` and `NIN` expression types. It delegates to `FilterHelper` to expand these expressions within a filter context. For other expression types, it falls back to the superclass's implementation.
relationship: This class is a specialized converter within a filter expression processing pipeline. It works with `Expression` objects and interacts with `FilterHelper` to expand specific filter types (`IN` and `NIN`). It's part of a larger system for building and applying metadata filters to vector stores, as used by components like `VectorStoreDocumentRetriever` and `SearchRequest`.
exampleUsage: This converter is used internally by the filter expression processing logic to handle `IN` and `NIN` operators when converting filter expressions into a format suitable for the underlying vector store. It's not directly instantiated or used by application code, but rather is invoked as part of the expression evaluation process. Example: A `SearchRequest` might contain a filter expression like `author in ['john', 'jill']`, which would be processed by this converter during the conversion to a vector store-specific filter.

description: SimpleVectorStore is a basic implementation of a vector store that provides functionalities to add, delete, and search vectors based on similarity. It supports saving and loading vector data from files in JSON format. It extends AbstractObservationVectorStore and utilizes an embedding model to generate vector representations of documents.
relationship: This class is part of the spring-ai-vector-store module and interacts with components like EmbeddingModel for generating embeddings, Document for representing data, and SearchRequest for defining search parameters. It can be used with other Spring AI modules like spring-ai-client-chat and spring-ai-rag for building conversational AI applications and RAG pipelines. It also relates to various vector database implementations like MongoDB, OpenSearch, and Pinecone through the VectorStore interface.
exampleUsage: 1. **Initialization:** Use the `SimpleVectorStore.builder(embeddingModel)` method to create a builder instance, providing an `EmbeddingModel`.  2. **Adding Documents:** Use the `doAdd` method to add a list of `Document` objects to the store. The embedding for each document is generated using the provided `EmbeddingModel`. 3. **Similarity Search:** Use the `doSimilaritySearch` method with a `SearchRequest` object to perform a similarity search. The request specifies the query, top-k results, and similarity threshold. 4. **Saving and Loading:** Use the `save` and `load` methods to persist the vector store's content to a file and load it from a file, respectively.

description: Represents the 'where' clause context in a filter expression, as defined by the Filters grammar. It parses and holds information about the WHERE keyword and the associated boolean expression.
relationship: This class is part of an ANTLR4-generated parser for filter expressions used with a vector database's similarity search functionality. It works in conjunction with `BooleanExpressionContext` and `FiltersListener`/`FiltersVisitor` to process and evaluate filter conditions applied to document metadata. It's used by the `Filter.Expression` class to provide a DSL for filtering search results.
exampleUsage: This class is not directly instantiated but is a result of parsing a filter expression string. It's used internally by the parser to represent the 'where' clause.  For example, if a filter expression is `country == 'UK' && year > 2020`, the parser will create a `WhereContext` instance to represent the `WHERE` part and a `BooleanExpressionContext` to represent the condition itself.

description: This class, `PrintFilterExpressionConverter`, is responsible for converting an `Expression` object into a human-readable string representation suitable for display or debugging. It handles different expression components like operands, operators, and grouping.
relationship: This class extends `AbstractFilterExpressionConverter` and is likely part of a larger system for processing and representing filter expressions. It doesn't directly relate to the tool-related classes (like `MethodToolCallback`, `ToolDefinition`, etc.) described in the documentation, but it could be used within a tool to format its input or output.
exampleUsage: The primary use case is to traverse an expression tree and build a string representation.  For example, to convert an expression, you would create an instance of this class and call its `doExpression` method with the expression and a `StringBuilder` to accumulate the result. The `doKey`, `doStartGroup`, and `doEndGroup` methods handle specific expression elements.

description: This class is a base visitor for a parse tree generated by a `FiltersParser`. It provides empty implementations for all visit methods defined in the `FiltersVisitor` interface, simply delegating to the default `visitChildren` method. This allows developers to easily create custom visitors that only need to handle a specific subset of filter expression types.
relationship: This class is part of a filter expression evaluation system, likely used to process metadata filters for similarity searches in a vector database. It works with the output of an ANTLR4-based parser (`FiltersParser`) and implements the `FiltersVisitor` interface. The documentation mentions `Filter.Expression` which this class helps to process.
exampleUsage: Extend this class to create a custom visitor to handle specific filter expression types. Override the relevant `visit...` methods to implement the desired logic for processing each expression. For example, to handle only 'Where' expressions, override only the `visitWhere` method.

description: The `MultiQueryExpander` class expands a given query into multiple semantically diverse variations using a large language model. This is useful for improving search recall by capturing different perspectives and increasing the chances of finding relevant information.
relationship: This class implements the `QueryExpander` interface and relies on a `ChatClient` to interact with the language model. It can be used in conjunction with other query transformation components like `TranslationQueryTransformer` to further refine search queries.
exampleUsage:
To use the `MultiQueryExpander`, first build an instance using the `builder()` method, providing a `ChatClient` builder and optionally customizing the number of queries to generate and whether to include the original query. Then, call the `expand()` method with the original `Query` to obtain a list of expanded queries.

Example:
```java
MultiQueryExpander expander = MultiQueryExpander.builder()
    .chatClientBuilder(chatClientBuilder)
    .numberOfQueries(3)
    .build();
List<Query> queries = expander.expand(new Query("How to run a Spring Boot app?"));
```

To exclude the original query:
```java
MultiQueryExpander expander = MultiQueryExpander.builder()
    .chatClientBuilder(chatClientBuilder)
    .includeOriginal(false)
    .build();
```

description: The RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor class implements common Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) flows, orchestrating query transformation, expansion, document retrieval, joining, post-processing, and augmentation before interacting with a chat model. It enhances prompts with relevant context retrieved from a document store.
relationship: This class is a core component of the `spring-ai-rag` module, interacting with interfaces like `QueryTransformer`, `QueryExpander`, `DocumentRetriever`, `DocumentJoiner`, `DocumentPostProcessor`, and `QueryAugmenter`. It integrates with the `ChatClient` through the `Advisor` interface, and utilizes `TaskExecutor` and `Scheduler` for asynchronous operations. It's part of a broader ecosystem including `spring-ai-commons`, `spring-ai-model`, `spring-ai-vector-store`, and `spring-ai-client-chat`.
exampleUsage:
To use the `RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor`, build an instance using the `Builder`, configuring components like `DocumentRetriever` (e.g., `VectorStoreDocumentRetriever`) and optionally `QueryExpander` and `DocumentPostProcessor`. Then, add the advisor to a `ChatClient`'s prompt using the `advisors()` method before calling the chat model. Example:

```java
RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor retrievalAugmentationAdvisor = RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor.builder()
        .documentRetriever(VectorStoreDocumentRetriever.builder()
                .similarityThreshold(0.50)
                .vectorStore(vectorStore)
                .build())
        .build();

String answer = chatClient.prompt()
        .advisors(retrievalAugmentationAdvisor)
        .user(question)
        .call()
        .content();
```

description: The `VectorStoreObservationContext` class is a data transfer object (DTO) used to encapsulate information about operations performed on a vector store. It holds details like the database system, operation name (add, query, delete, etc.), collection name, dimensions, field name, namespace, and similarity metric. It's designed to be used with observability frameworks to track and monitor vector store interactions.
relationship: This class is a core component of the Spring AI project, specifically within the `spring-ai-vector-store` module. It's used by `VectorStore` implementations to provide context for observability, allowing for metrics and tracing of vector store operations. It integrates with Micrometer for metric collection and tracing. It's also used in the `DemoApplication` example to demonstrate vector store usage.
exampleUsage:
To create an instance of `VectorStoreObservationContext`, use the builder pattern. Always provide a non-null and non-empty database system and operation name. Optional fields like collection name, dimensions, field name, namespace, and similarity metric can be set as needed. Example:

```java
VectorStoreObservationContext context = VectorStoreObservationContext.builder("my_db", VectorStoreObservationContext.Operation.QUERY)
    .collectionName("my_collection")
    .similarityMetric("cosine")
    .build();
```

description: The `FilterExpressionConverter` interface is responsible for converting a generic `Filter.Expression` into a string representation suitable for a specific vector store's query language. This allows for portable filter expressions to be translated into a format understood by the underlying database.
relationship: This interface is used in conjunction with `Filter.Expression` to provide a flexible way to filter data in vector stores. It's a key component in the `SearchRequest` and `VectorStoreDocumentRetriever` classes, enabling metadata filtering during similarity searches. It interacts with various vector store implementations (MongoDBAtlasVectorStore, PgVector, OracleVectorStore, Milvus) to translate the filter expression into their specific query formats.
exampleUsage:
Implementations of this interface are used to adapt filter expressions to different vector store technologies. The primary use case is within a `VectorStoreDocumentRetriever` to apply metadata filters during a similarity search.  You can either provide a filter expression as a string directly in the `SearchRequest` or programmatically using `Filter.Expression` and a `FilterExpressionBuilder`. For example:

```java
FilterExpressionBuilder b = new FilterExpressionBuilder();
vectorStore.similaritySearch(SearchRequest.builder()
    .query("The World")
    .topK(TOP_K)
    .similarityThreshold(SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD)
    .filterExpression(b.and(
        b.in("author","john", "jill"),
        b.eq("article_type", "blog")).build()).build());
```

description: The `Builder` class is used to construct a `VectorStoreObservationContext` object, which encapsulates configuration details for vector store operations. It provides a fluent interface for setting properties like database system, operation name, collection name, dimensions, field name, namespace, query request, query response, and similarity metric.
relationship: This class is a helper class for creating `VectorStoreObservationContext` objects, which are likely used within a larger vector store implementation (like `CosmosDBVectorStore`) to provide observability and context for interactions with the vector store. It's part of the `spring-ai-vector-store` module and interacts with components like `SearchRequest` and `Document` from `spring-ai-commons`.
exampleUsage:
The primary use case is to create a `VectorStoreObservationContext` with specific configuration details.  For example:

```java
VectorStoreObservationContext context = new VectorStoreObservationContext.Builder("CosmosDB", "similaritySearch")
    .collectionName("myCollection")
    .dimensions(1536)
    .queryRequest(SearchRequest.builder().query("example query").build())
    .build();
```

This allows for structured logging and monitoring of vector store interactions.

Spring AI builds upon the observability features in the Spring ecosystem to provide insights into AI-related operations.

The spring-boot-actuator module is required for enabling observability. Add the Spring Boot Actuator dependency to your project’s Maven pom.xml build file:

<dependency>
 <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
 <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-actuator</artifactId>
</dependency>

or to your Gradle build.gradle build file.

dependencies {
    implementation 'org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter-actuator'
}

Spring AI provides metrics and tracing capabilities for its core components: ChatClient (including Advisor), ChatModel, EmbeddingModel, ImageModel, and VectorStore.

Note
 Low cardinality keys will be added to metrics and traces, while high cardinality keys will only be added to traces.

It utilizes `VectorStoreObservationContext` to access query results and logs them using `LoggerFactory`. It integrates with Micrometer for observation and logging, and is used to provide insights into Vector Store operations.
exampleUsage: Implement this handler to log the content of Vector Store query responses.  Ensure the `spring-boot-starter-actuator` dependency is included for observability features. Configure logging levels to control the verbosity of the logged responses. The handler is automatically used when a `VectorStore` is observed.

description: This class is a base listener for the `FiltersListener` interface, providing empty implementations for all its methods. It serves as a convenient starting point for creating custom listeners that only need to handle a specific subset of filter events.
relationship: This class is related to the `FiltersListener` interface and the `FiltersParser` generated by ANTLR4. It's used to listen for and react to events during the parsing of filter expressions, which are used to filter documents based on their metadata. For example, you might extend this class to handle only `enterCompareExpression` and `exitCompareExpression` if you are only interested in comparison operations within the filter expression.

description: AbstractVectorStoreBuilder is an abstract base class for building VectorStore instances. It provides default implementations for observation-related settings and enforces the configuration of an EmbeddingModel. It supports method chaining through a generic type parameter and provides methods for configuring observation registries, custom observation conventions, and batching strategies.
relationship: This class serves as a base class for concrete VectorStore builders like MongoDBAtlasVectorStoreBuilder, MariaDBVectorStoreBuilder, OpenSearchVectorStoreBuilder, etc. It implements the VectorStore.Builder interface and is used to configure and create VectorStore instances. It interacts with EmbeddingModel and BatchingStrategy interfaces.
exampleUsage: Extend AbstractVectorStoreBuilder to create concrete builders for specific vector stores. Implement the necessary configuration options for the target vector store within the subclass. Use the fluent API (e.g., observationRegistry(), customObservationConvention(), batchingStrategy()) to configure the builder and then build() to create the VectorStore instance. Ensure an EmbeddingModel is provided during construction.

description: This class, `SimpleVectorStoreContent`, is designed to encapsulate content (text, metadata, and embedding) for use in a vector store. It provides a way to create a `Document` object from this content, which is likely used for similarity searches and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) applications.
relationship: This class is a core component for interacting with various vector stores (MongoDBAtlasVectorStore, MariaDBVectorStore, OpenSearchVectorStore, etc.) within the Spring AI project. It acts as a data transfer object (DTO) to represent the content that will be stored and retrieved from these vector stores. It's used in conjunction with interfaces like `VectorStore` and `VectorStoreRetriever` to perform vector-based searches.
exampleUsage: The primary use case is to create instances of `SimpleVectorStoreContent` with text, metadata, and a numerical embedding, then convert it into a `Document` object. The tests demonstrate creating content, validating its properties, and ensuring metadata immutability. It's important to provide a non-null and non-empty ID and embedding vector when creating an instance. The `toDocument()` method is used to create a `Document` object with a specified score.

description: The `ExpressionType` enum defines the types of filter expressions that can be used to query and filter data. It supports logical operations (AND, OR, NOT), comparison operations (EQ, NE, GT, GTE, LT, LTE), and inclusion/exclusion operations (IN, NIN).
relationship: This enum is used by the `Filter.Expression` class to represent the type of expression being evaluated. It's also used within the `SearchRequest` class to define filter criteria for similarity searches in vector stores. It interacts with vector store implementations like `MongoDBAtlasVectorStore`, `OracleVectorStore`, and `Weaviate VectorStore` to filter data based on metadata.
exampleUsage: The `ExpressionType` enum is primarily used to construct filter expressions for querying vector stores. For example, to filter documents where `docId` equals 'AIML-001' and `version` equals '1.0', you would create a nested `Filter.Expression` using `AND`, `EQ`, `Filter.Key`, and `Filter.Value`. Alternatively, string-based filter expressions can be used directly with the `filterExpression` parameter in `SearchRequest` or the `delete` method of a vector store.

description: This class is a static inner Builder class for the `RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor`. It provides a fluent API to configure various components of the advisor, such as query transformers, query expander, document retriever, document joiner, document post-processors, query augmenter, task executor, scheduler, and execution order.
relationship: The `Builder` class is tightly coupled with the `RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor` class. It's used to construct instances of `RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor` with specific configurations. It interacts with interfaces like `QueryTransformer`, `QueryExpander`, `DocumentRetriever`, `DocumentJoiner`, `DocumentPostProcessor`, `QueryAugmenter`, `TaskExecutor`, and `Scheduler`. It's part of the `spring-ai-rag` module, which provides a framework for Retrieval Augmented Generation pipelines.
exampleUsage:
The Builder pattern is used to create a `RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor` instance with a specific configuration.  You chain method calls to set the desired components and their order. For example:

```java
RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor advisor = RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor.builder()
    .queryTransformers(List.of(transformer1, transformer2))
    .documentRetriever(retriever)
    .build();
```

This allows for flexible and configurable RAG pipelines.

description: This class, `BooleanExpressionContext`, is a parser rule context generated by ANTLR4, specifically for handling boolean expressions within a filter DSL. It's part of the system for filtering documents based on their metadata.
relationship: This class is related to the `Filter.Expression` class and the external DSL built on ANTLR4 that parses filter expressions. It's used in conjunction with the `similaritySearch` methods of a vector database to filter documents based on metadata key-value pairs.
exampleUsage: This class is not directly instantiated or used by developers. It's an internal component of the ANTLR4-based filter expression parser. Developers interact with the filter functionality by providing filter expressions as strings (e.g., `country == 'UK' && year >= 2020 && isActive == true`) which are then parsed using this context.

description: The `Expression` record represents a boolean expression used for filtering data, particularly within the context of vector databases. It defines an expression type and two operands, allowing for comparisons, logical operations (AND/OR), and inclusion checks. It implements the `Operand` interface.
relationship: This class is central to the filtering mechanism used in `SearchRequest` and `VectorStoreDocumentRetriever`. It's used to define metadata filters for vector store searches. It interacts with `FilterExpressionBuilder` for programmatic construction and can be represented as a string for direct use in text-based filter expressions. It's also used by various vector store implementations (CassandraVectorStore, ChromaVectorStore, Milvus, OracleVectorStore) to translate filter expressions into their specific query formats.
exampleUsage:
The primary use case is to create filter expressions for refining vector store searches.  You can build expressions programmatically using `FilterExpressionBuilder`:

```java
FilterExpressionBuilder b = new FilterExpressionBuilder();
Expression expression = b.eq("country", "BG").build();
```

Or, you can use string-based expressions directly within a `SearchRequest`:

```java
vectorStore.similaritySearch(
    SearchRequest.builder()
    .query("The World")
    .filterExpression("author in ['john', 'jill'] && article_type == 'blog'").build());
```

Expressions can be combined using AND, OR, and other operators (EQUALS, GT, LT, IN, NIN, NOT).
